findings.statement: Intel Gaudi 3 delivers roughly 3.06 matrix TFLOPS per watt (1,835 TFLOPS / 600W), a search-computed efficiency figure.
findings.why: Perf-per-watt frames how competitive Intel's accelerator is against NVIDIA on efficiency, the key product attribute buyers weigh.

impact.mechanism: A more efficient Intel accelerator strengthens its appeal as an alternative, modestly pressuring NVIDIA's demand moat.

evidence.source: WebSearch synthesis - Intel Gaudi 3 specifications and perf-per-watt (Tom's Hardware, Intel white paper, Supermicro brief)
evidence.excerpt: approximately 3.06 TFLOPS per watt for matrix operations (1,835 TFLOPS / 600W)

confidence.basis: Derived figure from secondary search summaries; PDF/spec pages returned 403 and were not directly verified.

findings.statement: Gaudi 3 is positioned as a cheaper alternative to NVIDIA's H100 (slower, but Intel claims up to 1.7x training, 50% better inference, 40% better efficiency).
findings.why: A credible cheaper accelerator expands non-NVIDIA supply options for buyers.

impact.mechanism: Availability of a lower-cost competing accelerator adds alternative supply to the AI compute market.

evidence.source: WebSearch synthesis - Intel Gaudi 3 specifications and perf-per-watt (Tom's Hardware, Intel white paper, Supermicro brief)
evidence.excerpt: Slower than Nvidia's H100 AI GPU, but also cheaper

confidence.basis: Secondary coverage / vendor comparative claims, not verified against primary Intel docs.

competitiveStructure.rationale: Crescent Island and MI350-vs-GB200 expand alternative supply while custom ASICs grow 44.6%.

moat.rationale: NVIDIA share eroding 87%->81%->75% as AMD climbs toward 10-15%.

momentum.rationale: NVIDIA DC revenue $75.2B up 92% and networking up 199%, with AMD +57% and Intel DCAI +22%.

unitEconomics.rationale: NVIDIA ~75% gross margin with AMD 53-55% and Intel 41% supports robust economics.

narrative: Demand momentum is exceptional, led by NVIDIA's record $75.2B data-center quarter and broad-based growth at AMD and Intel. Margins remain healthy across vendors, but the competitive field is filling in with Intel's Crescent Island, AMD's MI350, and surging custom ASICs. NVIDIA's dominance is intact yet measurably eroding, so the durable moat is the softest part of the picture.
